>
> **Unlocking FPGA Innovation with Tang Nano 20K: Features, Advantages, and Applications** [nextpcb.com](https://www.nextpcb.com/blog/unlocking-fpga-innovation-with-tang-nano-20k-features-advantages-and-applications)
>

>
> **Sipeed Launches Its Tang Nano 20K FPGA Dev Board with a Linux Demo and NES Emulator Bundle - Hackster.io** [hackster.io](https://www.hackster.io/news/sipeed-launches-its-tang-nano-20k-fpga-dev-board-with-a-linux-demo-and-nes-emulator-bundle-ae3c972b2c39)
>
> **MiSTery on Tang Nano - Atari-Forum** [atari-forum.com](https://www.atari-forum.com/viewtopic.php?t=43080)
>
Not quite — nobody has built the full all-in-one sound processor that Reddit post describes on the Tang Nano 20K specifically. But there's a lot of related activity worth knowing about:

**What HAS been done on the Tang Nano 20K:**

- **C64Nano** — a full Commodore 64 core with MIDI support (including multiple MIDI interface types selectable from the OSD), which means the SID chip audio is there
- **WonderTANG** — an MSX cartridge using the Tang Nano 20K, implementing SCC+ sound (Konami's wavetable chip) along with memory mappers
- **MiSTeryNano** — a full Atari ST/STE core running on the $28 Tang Nano 20K, which includes the YM2149 sound chip

**Why the full vision hasn't happened yet:**

The Tang Nano 20K only has ~20K LUTs. Cramming OPL/OPN + Soundblaster + MIDI + DSP + multiple console audio cores all into one chip simultaneously would exceed its capacity. The bigger Sipeed boards (Mega 60K, Mega 138K) would be much better candidates for something like that.

**Closest thing to what was described** would be the MiSTer project on larger FPGAs, which already has many of those audio cores — the Tang ecosystem is still catching up.

If you're specifically chasing OPL2/OPL3 (Soundblaster FM) on a Tang Nano 20K, that's plausible as a standalone project since OPL2 cores exist in HDL (like jt49, jtopl from JT cores), but I couldn't find anyone who has packaged it up for the Tang Nano 20K specifically yet.



## Prompt:
3/26/2026, 8:29:35 PM

What could I do with it



## Response:
3/26/2026, 8:29:46 PM

Based on what's already been ported and what the hardware supports, here are the most practical things you can do with a Tang Nano 20K:

**Retro Computer Cores (ready to flash now)**
- Commodore 64 (C64Nano) — full system with SID audio and MIDI
- Atari ST/STE (MiSTeryNano) — full system with YM2149 sound
- Commodore VIC-20 (VIC20Nano)
- MSX with SCC+ sound (WonderTANG cartridge)

**Gaming**
- NES emulator — ships as an official demo from Sipeed
- SNES and other retro consoles have community cores in various states

**Development/Learning**
- Run a RISC-V soft-core CPU
- Boot actual Linux (via LiteX + VexRiscv)
- Learn VHDL/Verilog on cheap hardware (~$25–30)

**Audio specifically** (since that's what the post was about)
- Individual sound chip cores like YM2149, SID, SCC+ already exist within the system cores above
- A standalone OPL2/OPL3 core is theoretically doable but would need porting work — nobody's packaged one for the Tang Nano 20K yet as far as I can find

**The honest limitation** is that 20K LUTs fills up fast. You can run one good retro system at a time, not a mega all-in-one audio processor as the Reddit post imagined. If that's your goal, the Tang **Mega 60K or 138K** would give you much more room to work with.

What's your main interest — gaming, audio, learning FPGA development, or something else?



## Prompt:
3/26/2026, 8:31:24 PM

Idk just sound making I guess



## Response:
3/26/2026, 8:31:35 PM

For pure sound/music making, the Tang Nano 20K is actually a pretty fun fit. Here's what makes sense:

**Easiest starting points**
- Flash a C64 core and use it as a **SID synthesizer** — the SID chip has a huge following in the chiptune/synth world and sounds incredible
- Similarly, the YM2149 (Atari ST core) is a classic chip used in tons of electronic music

**More interesting rabbit holes**
- **Standalone OPL2 synth** — the FM synthesis chip from Sound Blaster cards. There are open HDL implementations (jtopl) that could be ported. That classic 80s/90s FM sound.
- **Chiptune instrument** — people build these into custom MIDI-controlled synths, essentially a hardware chiptune box you play from a keyboard
- **Generative/sequenced sound** — write your own simple sequencer in HDL that drives a sound core

**Realistic honest take**
If you just want to *make sound* without deep FPGA coding, the C64 SID route is the most rewarding for the least effort — there are ready-made cores, it sounds amazing, and there's a huge community around SID music.

If you want to eventually **design your own synth from scratch** in HDL, the Tang Nano 20K is great cheap hardware to learn on.

Do you play any instruments or use any DAWs currently? That'd help figure out what direction actually fits you.



## Prompt:
3/26/2026, 8:32:02 PM

I don't mind the fpga coding cause nobody's done it right, and it would be using Claude code anyways



## Response:
3/26/2026, 8:32:15 PM

That's a great attitude, and honestly Claude Code is well suited for HDL work. Here's how I'd think about approaching it:

**The gap that actually exists**
Nobody has made a clean, standalone, MIDI-controllable multi-chip sound module on cheap FPGA hardware. The closes things are buried inside full computer cores. A dedicated sound-focused project would be genuinely novel.

**Realistic scope for Tang Nano 20K**
You'd probably pick 1-2 sound chips to start rather than all of them at once:
- OPL2 (jtopl core exists, needs wiring up)
- SID (several open cores available)
- YM2151 (used in arcade games, great for FM)

Then wrap it with:
- MIDI input (pretty simple UART at 31250 baud)
- I2S or PWM audio output
- Maybe a simple UI over UART or HDMI

**How Claude Code would help**
It's actually quite good at Verilog/VHDL, can adapt existing open source cores, write testbenches, and debug timing issues. The hard part is usually integration and getting the clocking right — exactly the kind of thing it can iterate on with you.

**Honest starting point suggestion**
Pick one chip (OPL2 is probably most interesting/underserved), get MIDI in working, get audio out working, then expand. That alone would be a genuinely useful thing that doesn't really exist yet as a clean open project.

What sound aesthetic are you after — FM, wavetable, chiptune, or a mix?



## Prompt:
3/26/2026, 8:32:43 PM

Are there examples of these chips I can checkout idk



## Response:
3/26/2026, 8:32:55 PM

Yeah, the jt cores by Jose Tejada are the gold standard for open source sound chip implementations. Here's where to look:

**Jose Tejada's JT cores (GitHub: jotego)**
- `jtopl` — OPL2
- `jtopll` — OPL2 variant
- `jt51` — YM2151 (his most celebrated one, very accurate)
- `jt89` — SN76489 (Sega/BBC Micro)
- `jt49` — YM2149/AY-3-8910

These are the ones MiSTer uses. Very well written, actively maintained, and he has Patreon supporters funding accuracy work.

**SID cores**
- `sid6581` on GitHub — a few versions exist with varying accuracy
- The one inside C64Nano/MiSTer is probably the cleanest reference

**Good starting approach with Claude Code**
Pull down jt51 or jtopl, read the interface, then ask Claude Code to help you wire up a MIDI receiver → chip core → I2S output pipeline. The cores already handle the hard DSP math, you're mostly doing plumbing and timing.

**Worth reading first**
Jose's documentation is actually excellent — he explains the architecture of each chip which helps you understand what you're controlling via registers.

His GitHub is just `github.com/jotego` — everything is there. The jt51 readme in particular is a good starting read even if you don't use that chip first.

Which of those sounds most appealing to you sonically?



## Prompt:
3/26/2026, 8:33:18 PM

So what do I look up on YouTube. Idk I'm just trying to learn because I'm bored



## Response:
3/26/2026, 8:33:28 PM

Honestly for just getting started and not being bored, I'd search these:

**For the vibe/inspiration**
- "SID chip music" — just to hear what it sounds like
- "OPL2 FM synthesis" — same thing, hear the classic sound
- "jt51 FPGA" — see Jose Tejada talk about his work, he's a good presenter

**For actually learning**
- "Nandland FPGA basics" — best beginner FPGA YouTube channel, very clear
- "FPGA4Fun" — good practical projects
- "Verilog tutorial beginner" — if you want to get hands on fast

**For the Tang Nano specifically**
- Search "Tang Nano 20K" on YouTube — there's a growing number of project videos, mostly in the last year or two

**Honest suggestion though**
If you're just bored and curious, honestly just start watching Nandland. He makes FPGA stuff genuinely interesting and you'll go from zero to understanding how these sound cores actually work pretty fast. Then when you look at jotego's code it'll start making sense.

The other thing worth doing is just buying the Tang Nano 20K if you don't have one — they're like $20 on AliExpress. Having the hardware in your hand changes how motivated you feel to actually mess with it.

Do you already have one or are you still at the considering stage?



## Prompt:
3/26/2026, 8:35:27 PM

So if we started with the sid what would I do with it, like how would it play music



## Response:
3/26/2026, 8:35:37 PM

Good question, here's how the whole chain works:

**The basic signal flow**
MIDI keyboard/DAW → Tang Nano 20K → audio out → speakers/headphones

**What the SID chip actually does**
It has 3 voices, each with oscillator + filter + envelope. You write values to its registers to tell it things like pitch, waveform shape, volume, filter settings. It then generates audio.

**So your job as the FPGA programmer**
1. Receive MIDI messages (note on, note off, pitch etc)
2. Translate those into SID register writes
3. The SID core does the rest and outputs audio

**How you'd actually play music**
- **Live** — plug in a MIDI keyboard, play notes, each keypress triggers a voice
- **From a DAW** — Ableton/FL Studio etc send MIDI out to the device, you sequence it like any synth
- **SID files** — there's a huge archive of classic C64 music in .sid format, you could build a player that streams register data directly
- **Built-in sequencer** — write a simple pattern sequencer in the FPGA itself, no computer needed

**The coolest option honestly**
The .sid file player route — you'd have access to thousands of classic C64 songs playing on real hardware. There's a site called HVSC (High Voltage SID Collection) with like 50,000 of them.

Does any of those use cases jump out at you?



## Prompt:
3/26/2026, 8:35:54 PM

Are there cool synths made from fpgas



## Response:
3/26/2026, 8:36:09 PM

Yeah there are some really cool ones, let me search for the best examples.

````plaintext
Web Search: cool FPGA synthesizers projects 2024
````

> **futur3soundz | Pro Sound on FPGAs by René Ceballos** [futur3soundz.com](https://www.futur3soundz.com/)
>
> **fpga4fun.com - where FPGAs are fun** [fpga4fun.com](https://www.fpga4fun.com/)
>
> **FPGA platform recommendation for a synthesizer | __('.Stack') | Hackaday.io** [hackaday.io](https://hackaday.io/page/10924)
>
> **FPGA Synthesizer | BitBuilt - Giving Life to Old Consoles** [bitbuilt.net](https://bitbuilt.net/forums/threads/fpga-synthesizer.3385/)
>
> **FPGA based design for a synth feasible? - MOD WIGGLER** [modwiggler.com](https://www.modwiggler.com/forum/viewtopic.php?t=279038)
>
> **MATRIXSYNTH: FPGA** [matrixsynth.com](https://www.matrixsynth.com/search/label/FPGA)
>
> **XFM2 Synthesizer | 64-voice FM Synthesizer on FPGA | futur3soundz** [futur3soundz.com](https://www.futur3soundz.com/xfm2/)
>
> **XFM2 Is an FPGA-Based Synthesizer Module - Hackster.io** [hackster.io](https://www.hackster.io/news/xfm2-is-an-fpga-based-synthesizer-module-68a7979ef613)
>
> **A subtractive music synth fully implemented in FPGA - element14 Community** [element14.com](https://community.element14.com/technologies/fpga-group/b/blog/posts/a-subtractive-music-synth-fully-implemented-in-fpga)
>
> **XFM: A 32-Voice Polyphonic FM Synthesizer On An FPGA | Hackaday** [hackaday.com](https://hackaday.com/2019/07/24/xfm-a-32-voice-polyphonic-fm-synthesizer-on-an-fpga/)
>
Yeah there are some genuinely impressive ones. Here are the standouts:

**XFM2 by René Ceballos** — this is probably the coolest DIY one. It's a 64-voice, 6-operator FM synthesizer with a built-in effects processor, built on an FPGA chip. It has less than 0.5ms latency, 128 program memory slots, an arpeggiator, and 24-bit stereo output — total cost around $100. The code is all free. Definitely worth watching on YouTube.

**Tiliqua** — an open source Eurorack module aimed at making FPGA audio synthesis accessible, supporting extreme oversampling, low-latency effects, video synthesis, and high-speed USB audio. You can store up to 8 projects simultaneously and switch between them without a PC. Very modern and polished.

**Commercial ones** — the Waldorf Kyra, UDO Super 6, and Novation Peak are all FPGA based commercial synths. The Super 6 especially sounds incredible and has a big following.

**What's interesting for you specifically** is that René's XFM2 is basically proof that one person with an FPGA can build something competitive with commercial hardware. His site is `futur3soundz.com` and is worth a deep read.

Search "XFM2 synthesizer demo" on YouTube — hearing it is more convincing than reading about it.



## Prompt:
3/26/2026, 8:38:46 PM

What about guitar



## Response:
3/26/2026, 8:39:04 PM

Good pivot — FPGA and guitar is actually a really interesting space.

````plaintext
Web Search: FPGA guitar effects pedal projects open source 2024
````

> **GitHub - Vladilit/fpga-multi-effect: FPGA-based Multi-Effects system for the electric guitar · GitHub** [github.com](https://github.com/Vladilit/fpga-multi-effect)
>
> **GitHub - carsonrobles/fpga-guitar-pedal: FPGA based guitar effect station.** [github.com](https://github.com/carsonrobles/fpga-guitar-pedal)
>
> **GitHub - jsamarth/GuitarPedalBoard: This is a FPGA Project on Guitar Effects. Usually, guitarists use one guitar effect Stompbox per effect. This project aimed at using the FPGA hardware, which is significantly faster than Microcontrollers, to modulate Guitar signals being fed into the board using Digital Signal Processing.** [github.com](https://github.com/jsamarth/GuitarPedalBoard)
>
> **"An FPGA Implementation of Digital Guitar Effects" by Carson James Robles** [calpoly.edu](https://digitalcommons.calpoly.edu/cpesp/316/)
>
> **FXcursion | Hackaday.io** [hackaday.io](https://hackaday.io/project/192448-fxcursion)
>
> **An FPGA Implementation of Digital Guitar Effects A Senior Project Report** [calpoly.edu](https://digitalcommons.calpoly.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi?article=1326&context=cpesp)
>
> **GitHub - vincentgosselin1/FPGA_Guitar_pedal: Guitar pedal I made for my FPGA (altera) class at school.** [github.com](https://github.com/vincentgosselin1/FPGA_Guitar_pedal)
>
> **Guitar-Pedal-Effects-on-FPGA/README.md at master · jagumiel/Guitar-Pedal-Effects-on-FPGA** [github.com](https://github.com/jagumiel/Guitar-Pedal-Effects-on-FPGA/blob/master/README.md)
>
> **GitHub - jagumiel/Guitar-Pedal-Effects-on-FPGA: This is my bachelor's degree project. I have used a FPGA to modify audio signal and create different sounds, like distortion, overdrive, echo, reverb...** [github.com](https://github.com/jagumiel/Guitar-Pedal-Effects-on-FPGA)
>
> **myMadFX: Internet-Enabled Guitar Effects Pedal - NI Community** [ni.com](https://forums.ni.com/t5/Student-Projects/myMadFX-Internet-Enabled-Guitar-Effects-Pedal/ta-p/3537119)
>
Guitar and FPGA is actually a really natural fit. Here's the landscape:

**Why FPGA is great for guitar effects**
Ultra low latency is the big one — regular computers and even microcontrollers introduce lag that feels wrong when playing. FPGA processes audio in near-real-time which is exactly what you want.

**What people have built**
Lots of uni projects doing the basics — distortion, overdrive, tremolo, delay, and octaver effects are the most common. The more ambitious ones pack five effects into one device with tunable parameters, essentially replacing a whole pedalboard.

**The more interesting stuff**
FXcursion is an open source project doing recording, looping, effects processing, and mixing for up to 6 inputs in a guitar pedal form factor — basically a full standalone rig.

**What's genuinely missing and interesting**
Nobody has done a Tang Nano 20K guitar pedal with amp simulation (like modelling a Marshall or Fender circuit in the FPGA). That's the holy grail for FPGA guitar — the Kemper and Quad Cortex do this on DSPs for thousands of dollars. A cheap open FPGA version would be a big deal.

**Practical reality for Tang Nano 20K**
You'd need a small ADC/DAC board to get the guitar signal in and out — something like a PCM5102 DAC (~$5 on AliExpress) and a PCM1802 ADC. Then the FPGA does all the processing in between.

Do you actually play guitar or is this more of a curiosity?



## Prompt:
3/26/2026, 8:39:44 PM

Nobody's done something like that ? Why



## Response:
3/26/2026, 8:40:30 PM

Honestly a few reasons:

**The hard technical parts**
Amp simulation (convolution reverb, tube saturation modelling) is computationally heavy even for DSPs. On an FPGA you're doing it in hardware logic which means designing the actual math circuits, not just writing software. Most FPGA hobbyists are engineers/CS people who don't play guitar, and most guitarists don't know FPGAs.

**The tooling gap until recently**
FPGA development used to require expensive proprietary tools and deep expertise. It's only really in the last 2-3 years that cheap boards like the Tang Nano and open source toolchains have made it accessible to regular hobbyists.

**The community is fragmented**
Guitar DSP people hang out in different places than FPGA people. They rarely overlap. The Reddit post you screenshotted is basically someone standing in that gap going "why hasn't anyone done this."

**Commercial incentive works against it**
Kemper, Line 6, Fractal — these companies have massive incentive to keep this stuff proprietary and expensive. There's no VC money funding open source amp sim.

**Why NOW might be the right time though**
- Cheap capable boards like Tang Nano exist
- Claude Code can help non-experts write HDL
- Open source IR (impulse response) amp captures are freely available online
- The MiSTer community proved hobbyists can do serious FPGA work

Honestly the combination of you being bored, having a Tang Nano, and Claude Code existing is kind of exactly the conditions where something like this gets built for the first time.



## Prompt:
3/26/2026, 8:41:10 PM

So how would you emulate them



## Response:
3/26/2026, 8:41:24 PM

Great question. There are basically two approaches:

**Approach 1: Impulse Response (IR) Convolution**
The simpler one conceptually. You take a "snapshot" of how a real amp/cabinet responds to sound, stored as an IR file. Then you mathematically convolve your guitar signal with that IR in real time. This is how the Kemper works at its core. Thousands of free IR files exist online for Marshalls, Fenders, Mesa Boogie etc.

The FPGA does fast convolution math on the incoming audio samples. It's heavy on multiply-accumulate operations but FPGAs are actually really good at that since they have dedicated DSP blocks.

**Approach 2: Circuit Modelling**
The harder, more interesting one. You actually model the behavior of the physical circuit — the tubes, transformers, capacitors. Every component becomes a math equation running in the FPGA. This is what Fractal Audio does and why their stuff sounds so real.

The Tang Nano 20K would struggle with full circuit modelling — it's probably a bigger board job. But IR convolution is realistic.

**The actual pipeline would be**
```
Guitar → ADC → FPGA applies IR convolution → DAC → speaker/headphones
```

**What makes it hard in practice**
The IR convolution needs to happen faster than the audio sample period — around 22 microseconds at 44.1kHz. Timing everything correctly in hardware is the tricky part.

**The exciting bit**
IR files are just data. You could load different ones from an SD card and switch amp models on the fly. Marshall one second, Fender the next.

Want to dig into what that would actually look like in Verilog?



## Prompt:
3/26/2026, 8:42:34 PM

Sure seems fun but approach two is better right ? Talk about both I guess



## Response:
3/26/2026, 8:43:00 PM

Yeah approach two is definitely the more interesting one. Let me break both down properly.

**Approach 1: IR Convolution**

The math is called FIR filtering. Your guitar signal comes in as digital samples, and you're basically doing this repeatedly:

```
output = sample[0]*IR[0] + sample[1]*IR[1] + sample[2]*IR[2] ... 
```

Hundreds or thousands of multiplications per audio sample. FPGAs are genuinely great at this because you can do many multiplications simultaneously in parallel — a CPU does them one at a time sequentially.

The IR captures the *linear* behavior of an amp — the frequency response, the cabinet coloring, the room. What it **can't** capture is the nonlinear stuff — the way tubes compress and saturate when you dig in hard, the feel of the amp responding to your playing dynamics. That's why IR-only solutions sound a bit flat and lifeless to serious players.

**Approach 2: Circuit Modelling (WDF — Wave Digital Filters)**

This is where it gets genuinely deep. The technique used in serious amp modelling is called Wave Digital Filters. The idea is you take a real circuit schematic and mathematically transform each component into a digital equivalent:

- Resistors become simple gain stages
- Capacitors become first order filters with memory
- Tubes (the hard part) become nonlinear functions that need iterative solving

Every component in the signal chain gets modelled. A typical preamp stage might be:

```
Input → coupling cap → grid resistor → triode tube model → 
plate resistor → coupling cap → next stage
```

Each of those is running as math every single sample period.

**Why tubes are the hard part**

Tubes are nonlinear — their behavior changes depending on the signal level. At low volumes they're relatively clean, as you push them they compress and saturate in a very musical way. That saturation is literally what "warm tube sound" means.

Modelling this requires solving implicit equations — the output depends on the input in a circular way that needs iterative math to resolve. On a CPU you just loop until it converges. On an FPGA you need to figure out how many iterations you need and pipeline them in hardware.

**How they combine in practice**

The best approach actually uses both:

```
Guitar → preamp circuit model (tubes, tone stack) → 
power amp circuit model → IR convolution for cabinet → output
```

The circuit model handles all the nonlinear musical stuff — the saturation, the dynamics, the feel. The IR handles the cabinet and room which genuinely is linear and IR captures it perfectly.

**Feasibility on Tang Nano 20K**

Honestly a full circuit model of a Marshall JCM800 is probably pushing it for 20K LUTs. But a simpler amp — like a Fender Champ which has a very simple circuit — might be doable. And the Tang Mega 60K or 138K would open it up significantly.

The other realistic option is a hybrid approach — run the nonlinear tube modelling on the onboard BL616 microcontroller and do the convolution/filtering on the FPGA fabric itself. The Tang Nano 20K actually has both on board which is a underexplored combination.

**What Claude Code could realistically help with**

- Implementing the FIR convolution engine in Verilog
- Porting existing WDF implementations (there are academic ones in C++) to HDL
- Writing the ADC/DAC interface code
- Building a simple parameter control system

The WDF math itself is well documented in academic papers — Küpfmüller, Fettweis, and more recently Kurt Werner's PhD thesis is basically the bible for audio circuit modelling. All free online.

The gap that exists is nobody has taken that academic work and made it run on a $25 FPGA board. That's the interesting project.



## Prompt:
3/26/2026, 8:45:20 PM

So wdf is more pure ?



## Response:
3/26/2026, 8:45:33 PM

Yeah in a meaningful sense it is. Here's why:

**IR is a recording of behavior**
You're capturing how an amp responded once, in one condition, at one volume level. It's essentially a photograph. It doesn't know you're playing softly vs digging in hard — it applies the same response either way. That's the fundamental limitation.

**WDF is the actual physics**
You're modelling why the amp sounds the way it does from first principles. The circuit doesn't have a fixed response — it reacts to your signal dynamically the same way the real amp does. Play softly and the tubes barely saturate, play hard and they compress and break up. That interaction is what players mean when they talk about an amp "feeling alive" or "responding to touch."

**The philosophical difference**
IR says "this amp sounded like this"
WDF says "this amp *is* this, behave accordingly"

**Where it gets interesting**
With WDF you can do things that are physically impossible with real amps or IR captures:

- Turn a Marshall's plate voltage up beyond what the real amp can handle safely
- Swap tubes between amps that were never designed for them
- Create hybrid circuits that don't exist in the real world
- Dial in component values with infinite precision

You're not just emulating amps anymore — you're doing physics experiments on virtual circuits that would destroy real hardware.

**The catch**
WDF is significantly harder to implement and more computationally expensive. Which is exactly why nobody has done it cleanly on cheap FPGA hardware yet.

That's actually the compelling reason to pursue it — IR convolution is a solved problem commercially. WDF on a $25 board isn't.

3/26/2026, 8:46:16 PM

Okay let's do that the.

3/26/2026, 8:46:25 PM

````plaintext
Tool: Generating ask_user_input_v0...
````





## Prompt:
3/26/2026, 8:47:05 PM

Q: Do you have a Tang Nano 20K already?
A: Yes got one

## Response:
3/26/2026, 8:47:17 PM

Perfect, so you've got the board and you're not starting from zero. That's a solid position.

**Here's the realistic plan:**

**Phase 1 — Get audio in and out**
Before any amp modelling you need the hardware plumbing working. You'll need:
- A cheap I2S ADC to get guitar signal in (PCM1802 or similar, ~$3-5)
- A cheap I2S DAC to get audio out (PCM5102, very common, ~$5)
- Verilog I2S controller to talk to both

This is actually the most important and underrated step. Without clean low-latency audio I/O nothing else matters.

**Phase 2 — Simple nonlinearity first**
Before full WDF, implement a basic tube saturation function — just a soft clipping curve in a lookup table. Plug guitar in, get a distorted signal out. Proves the pipeline works end to end.

**Phase 3 — First WDF circuit**
Start with a single triode stage — the simplest meaningful tube circuit. One tube, one resistor, two caps. That's a Fender Champ preamp stage roughly. Get that sounding right before adding complexity.

**Phase 4 — Build outward**
Add tone stack, second gain stage, power amp stage. Each addition is incremental.

**First concrete thing to do right now**
Look up the PCM5102 and PCM1802 boards on AliExpress — they're dirt cheap and well documented. That's your immediate shopping list.

Want to start writing the I2S controller in Verilog, or do you want to read more about WDF first?



## Prompt:
3/26/2026, 8:48:00 PM

More about how to implement the wdf



## Response:
3/26/2026, 8:48:26 PM

Okay this gets pretty deep but I'll make it digestible.

**The core idea**

In a real circuit, components are connected by wires carrying voltage and current. WDF transforms those into "wave quantities" — incident and reflected waves, like sound bouncing around. Every component becomes a "port" that receives an incoming wave and sends back a reflected wave.

The math for each component ends up being surprisingly simple:

**Resistor**
```
reflected = 0  (just absorbs, no reflection)
```

**Capacitor**
```
reflected = incident + 2 * previous_state
state updates each sample
```

**Inductor** (transformers etc)
```
reflected = -incident + 2 * previous_state
```

These are trivially cheap in hardware. The problem is the tube.

**The Triode Problem**

A triode tube has three terminals — cathode, grid, plate. The relationship between them is described by the Koren equation:

```
Ip = (Vgk/mu + Vpk)^1.5 * some constants
```

Where Ip is plate current, Vgk is grid-cathode voltage, Vpk is plate-cathode voltage. The problem is Vpk depends on Ip which depends on Vpk — it's circular. This is the implicit nonlinearity everyone talks about.

**How you solve it in hardware**

Three main approaches:

**Option 1 — Newton-Raphson iteration**
Numerically solve the implicit equation each sample. Converges in 2-4 iterations usually. Expensive but accurate. Hard to pipeline cleanly in FPGA.

**Option 2 — Lookup Table (LUT)**
Pre-compute the tube's response across its entire operating range and store it in a table. At runtime just look up the answer. Very fast, very FPGA-friendly, slight accuracy tradeoff but honestly inaudible.

**Option 3 — K-method / Explicit approximation**
Mathematical tricks to reformulate the implicit equation into an explicit one — meaning the output doesn't depend on itself anymore. Kurt Werner's thesis covers this in depth. This is probably the best approach for FPGA because it's both accurate and cheap.

**The circuit tree structure**

WDF organises a circuit as a binary tree. Leaves are components, branches are junctions that combine waves. There are two junction types:

**Series junction**
```
Components sharing current
reflected = -incident + 2*(R1/(R1+R2)) * (incident1 + incident2)
```

**Parallel junction**
```
Components sharing voltage  
reflected = incident - 2*(G1/(G1+G2)) * (incident1 + incident2)
```

Where R is port resistance and G is conductance. These are just multiplications and additions — perfect for FPGA DSP blocks.

**What a single triode stage looks like as a tree**

```
         [Parallel junction]
        /                   \
[Series junction]        [Plate resistor]
   /         \
[Tube]    [Coupling cap]
              |
           [Grid resistor]
              |
           [Input]
```

You evaluate the tree bottom-up every sample period. Leaves compute first, junctions combine upward, result comes out the top.

**The FPGA pipeline**

Because the tree has a fixed depth you can pipeline it perfectly:

```
Sample arrives → 
Stage 1: compute leaf nodes simultaneously →
Stage 2: compute first level junctions →
Stage 3: compute root junction →
Output sample
```

Each stage takes one clock cycle. At 27MHz you have way more cycles than you need for 44.1kHz audio. Plenty of headroom.

**Where to read deeper**

Kurt Werner's 2016 Stanford PhD thesis "Virtual Analog Modelling of Audio Circuitry Using Wave Digital Filters" is the definitive resource. Free PDF online. Chapters 3 and 4 are the ones you want. It's dense but Claude Code can help you translate the math directly into Verilog.

**The honest implementation order**

1. Implement one resistor port — trivial
2. Implement one capacitor port — easy
3. Implement series and parallel junctions — straightforward
4. Implement the LUT tube model first — get it working
5. Replace LUT with K-method explicit model — get it accurate

Each step is testable independently which makes debugging manageable.

Want to start actually writing the first component in Verilog, or dig into the tube math more first?



## Prompt:
3/26/2026, 8:50:44 PM

Tube



## Response:
3/26/2026, 8:51:18 PM

Okay the tube is the heart of everything so worth really understanding it.

**What a triode actually does physically**

A triode has three elements inside a vacuum tube:
- **Cathode** — heated metal that emits electrons
- **Grid** — a mesh between cathode and plate, controls electron flow
- **Plate** — collects the electrons

The grid voltage is your guitar signal. Small changes in grid voltage cause large changes in plate current. That's amplification. But the relationship isn't linear — it curves, and at extremes it saturates. That curve is the sound.

**The Koren equation**

The most accurate and commonly used model for a triode:

```
Ed = Vpk/kp * log(1 + exp(kp * (1/mu + Vgk/sqrt(kvb + Vpk²))))

Ip = (Ed^ex / kg1) * (1 + sign(Ed))
```

Variables:
- `Vpk` — plate to cathode voltage
- `Vgk` — grid to cathode voltage (your signal)
- `mu, ex, kg1, kp, kvb` — constants specific to each tube type

**Real tube constants**

This is where it gets fun — each tube has its own character:

```
12AX7 (most common preamp tube, Marshall/Fender)
mu=100, ex=1.4, kg1=1060, kp=600, kvb=300

6L6 (Fender power amp, clean warm)
mu=8.7, ex=1.35, kg1=1460, kp=48, kvb=12

EL34 (Marshall power amp, aggressive)
mu=11, ex=1.35, kg1=650, kp=60, kvb=24

300B (audiophile single ended, very warm)
mu=3.9, ex=1.35, kg1=730, kp=300, kvb=450
```

Just swapping these constants gives you completely different amp characters. That's the power of WDF.

**The implicit problem in detail**

Here's why it's circular. In a real circuit with a plate resistor Rp:

```
Vpk = Vsupply - Ip * Rp
```

But Ip depends on Vpk via the Koren equation. So:

```
Vpk = Vsupply - Koren(Vpk) * Rp
```

Vpk is on both sides. You can't just solve it directly.

**The LUT approach in detail**

Pre-compute a 2D grid of Ip values across the operating range of Vgk and Vpk:

```
Vgk range: -4V to 0V (typical preamp)
Vpk range: 0V to 300V (typical plate supply)

For each combination, compute Ip via Koren equation
Store in a 2D array
```

At runtime, your incoming wave quantities tell you approximate Vgk and Vpk, you look up Ip, done. One clock cycle.

The tradeoff is memory — a 256x256 table of 16-bit values is 128KB. The Tang Nano 20K has 828KB of block RAM so it fits, but it eats a chunk of your resources.

**The K-method approach**

Kurt Werner's key insight was that you can reformulate the WDF equations so the tube's implicit equation becomes explicit — meaning you can solve it directly without iteration.

The trick is choosing port resistances carefully so the nonlinear element sees a Thevenin equivalent source. Then the tube equation becomes:

```
Vgk = a - R_port * Koren(Vgk)
```

Which you can solve with one Newton step if you have a good initial guess — and your previous sample is always a great initial guess because audio changes slowly relative to sample rate.

In practice this means:

```
// Previous sample as initial guess
Vgk_guess = Vgk_previous

// One Newton step
f = Vgk_guess - a + R_port * Koren(Vgk_guess)
df = 1 + R_port * dKoren/dVgk  // derivative, precomputed in LUT
Vgk_new = Vgk_guess - f/df

// That's accurate enough at 44.1kHz
```

One division, a couple of multiplications, one LUT lookup. Very manageable in FPGA.

**The derivative LUT**

You actually need two LUTs — one for Koren(Vgk, Vpk) and one for its derivative dIp/dVgk. Both precomputed offline in Python and loaded as Verilog memory initialisations.

```python
import numpy as np

def koren(Vpk, Vgk, mu, ex, kg1, kp, kvb):
    Ed = Vpk/kp * np.log(1 + np.exp(kp * (1/mu + Vgk/np.sqrt(kvb + Vpk**2))))
    Ip = (np.maximum(Ed, 0)**ex) / kg1
    return Ip

# Generate LUT
Vpk_range = np.linspace(0, 300, 256)
Vgk_range = np.linspace(-4, 0, 256)
lut = np.zeros((256, 256))

for i, vpk in enumerate(Vpk_range):
    for j, vgk in enumerate(Vgk_range):
        lut[i,j] = koren(vpk, vgk, 100, 1.4, 1060, 600, 300)
```

You'd run this Python offline once, output it as a Verilog `$readmemh` file, done.

**What this means sonically**

The beautiful thing is that at different signal levels the tube is hitting different parts of its curve:

- **Clean** — operating in the linear region, gentle amplification
- **Edge of breakup** — starting to hit the curve, subtle harmonic compression
- **Driven** — deep in saturation, rich even harmonics, that classic crunch
- **Pushed hard** — asymmetric clipping, odd harmonics, aggressive distortion

All of that emerges naturally from the math. You don't program "distortion" — you program physics and distortion happens.

**The most important insight**

The 12AX7 is in virtually every guitar amp ever made — Fender, Marshall, Vox, Mesa, all of them. It's the starting point. Get one 12AX7 triode stage sounding right and you've got the core of every famous amp in history.

Want to start writing the Python LUT generator, or go into how the triode stage connects to the rest of the WDF tree first?

## Response:
3/27/2026, 4:11:20 AM

## Response:
3/27/2026, 5:41:17 AM

Let me search to make sure I'm not misleading you.

````plaintext
Web Search: WDF wave digital filter tube amp FPGA Verilog implementation
````

> **Emulation of analog audio circuits on FPGA using wave digital filters | Request PDF** [researchgate.net](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/313013448_Emulation_of_analog_audio_circuits_on_FPGA_using_wave_digital_filters)
>
> **Emulation of analog audio circuits on FPGA using wave digital filters | Proceedings of the 2nd International Conference on Communication and Information Processing** [acm.org](https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/3018009.3018028)
>
> **3D wave digital filter implementation on a virtex2 FPGA board with external SDRAM | IEEE Conference Publication | IEEE Xplore** [ieee.org](https://ieeexplore.ieee.org/document/5191463/)
>
> **Wave digital filter based analog circuit emulation on FPGA | IEEE Conference Publication | IEEE Xplore** [ieee.org](https://ieeexplore.ieee.org/document/7527483/)
>
> **Implementing Filters on FPGAs Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering** [sdsu.edu](https://ashrafi.sdsu.edu/PDF/filters_FPGA.pdf)
>
> **Toward Wave Digital Filter based Analog Circuit Emulation on FPGA (Abstract Only) | Proceedings of the 2015 ACM/SIGDA International Symposium on Field-Programmable Gate Arrays** [acm.org](https://dl.acm.org/doi/abs/10.1145/2684746.2689143)
>
> **(PDF) Wave Digital Filter Adaptors for Arbitrary Topologies and Multiport Linear Elements** [academia.edu](https://www.academia.edu/17257287/Wave_Digital_Filter_Adaptors_for_Arbitrary_Topologies_and_Multiport_Linear_Elements)
>
> **(PDF) Wave Digital Filter Modeling of Circuits with Operational Amplifiers** [academia.edu](https://www.academia.edu/27559334/Wave_Digital_Filter_Modeling_of_Circuits_with_Operational_Amplifiers)
>
> **Optimized Implementation for Wave Digital Filter Based Circuit Emulation on FPGA** [bit.edu.cn](http://journal.bit.edu.cn/jbit/en/article/doi/10.15918/j.jbit1004-0579.201726.0213)
>
> **FPGA Crystal Oscillator Circuit Emulation Based on Wave Digital Filter | Request PDF** [researchgate.net](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/375735236_FPGA_Crystal_Oscillator_Circuit_Emulation_Based_on_Wave_Digital_Filter)
>
Okay so the honest answer is more nuanced than I implied:

**What has been done**
Academic papers exist implementing WDF on FPGA — including a guitar preamplifier specifically, implemented on National Instruments CompactRIO hardware using LabVIEW. And researchers have shown FPGA-based WDF can process analog signals sampled at up to 512kHz, well above what audio needs.

**But here's the real gap**
All of this work lives in academic papers, expensive research hardware like CompactRIO, and LabVIEW — none of it is:
- Open source Verilog you can actually use
- Running on a $25 hobbyist board
- Packaged as something a guitarist can plug into
- Documented in a way normal people can build on

There's also RT-WDF, an open source C++ library for real-time WDF modelling — but that runs on CPUs, not FPGAs.

**So the gap is real, just more specific**

The science is proven. The math is published. What doesn't exist is clean open Verilog that runs on cheap hardware that a person can actually use.

That's a much more achievable gap to fill than inventing new science. You'd essentially be translating known academic work into something real and usable. That's actually the best kind of project — the hard thinking is done, the implementation just hasn't happened yet.

Want to start with the Python LUT generator and the Verilog tree now?



## Prompt:
3/27/2026, 5:41:45 AM

In 2026



## Response:
3/27/2026, 5:41:56 AM

Yeah exactly — the timing is actually kind of perfect right now for a few reasons:

**Why 2026 specifically is the right moment**

The Tang Nano 20K only came out in 2023. The hobbyist FPGA community around cheap Gowin boards is still very young — most of the serious cores like MiSTeryNano are only 1-2 years old. The ecosystem is just hitting its stride.

Claude Code didn't exist a year ago. The ability for someone without a deep HDL background to iterate on Verilog quickly is genuinely new. That changes what's feasible for one person working alone.

**The RT-WDF C++ library exists**
That's actually huge. It means someone has already done the hard work of structuring the math correctly in code. Translating structured C++ to Verilog with Claude Code is very different from deriving it from scratch from academic papers.

**Nobody is working on this specific thing**
The academic people doing WDF research aren't guitar players and aren't targeting $25 boards. The guitar pedal FPGA people are doing basic distortion and delay, not circuit modelling. The hobbyist FPGA community is focused on retro gaming. You're genuinely sitting in a gap nobody is currently filling.

**The realistic outcome**
If you got even a single convincing 12AX7 preamp stage running on the Tang Nano 20K and posted it on GitHub with a demo video, it would blow up in the r/fpgagaming and guitar communities overnight. Nothing like it exists.

Want to actually start building it?

>

>

>

>

>
> **Slimmable NAM: The Future of Neural Amp Modeler · TONE3000** [tone3000.com](https://www.tone3000.com/blog/slimmable-nam-the-future-of-neural-amp-modeler)
>
> **GitHub - sdatkinson/neural-amp-modeler: Neural network emulator for guitar amplifiers. · GitHub** [github.com](https://github.com/sdatkinson/neural-amp-modeler)
>
Okay this is genuinely useful — the landscape is more interesting than I knew. Honest updated picture:

**What actually exists in March 2026**

FPGAmp on GitHub exists but it's basically a readme and a block diagram — zero stars, work in progress, no actual implementation. So that gap is real.

**The bigger thing you should know about: NAM**

Neural Amp Modeler is a completely different approach — instead of WDF physics modelling, it uses deep learning to capture how a real amp actually sounds by listening to recordings of it. And it's exploding right now. In 2025 it started showing up in affordable hardware pedals — one unit at $65 already supports it.

NAM A2 was announced with a March 2026 launch, specifically designed to be slimmable — meaning a single model can scale down automatically for embedded hardware with DSP constraints. That's literally this month.

**So the honest picture is:**

- WDF on cheap FPGA — still genuinely unexplored, your original idea stands
- NAM on FPGA — also unexplored, but NAM is a wildly active ecosystem with 275,000 amp captures already available for free

**The interesting question for you**

WDF is the "purer" physics approach we talked about. NAM is the AI approach — less philosophically pure but arguably sounds better in practice and has a massive free library of real amp captures.

Running NAM inference on FPGA fabric would also be novel and potentially more immediately useful to guitarists.

Which direction actually appeals to you more?

3/27/2026, 5:43:04 AM

So sticking with WDF — the research confirmed the gap is real. Academic papers prove it works on FPGA, but nobody has shipped clean open Verilog for it on cheap hardware.

You want to just start building? The logical first step is the Python LUT generator for the 12AX7 — it's self contained, you can run it right now without any hardware, and it produces the core data that everything else depends on.

## Response:
3/27/2026, 5:44:36 AM

Let me write it now.

````plaintext
Create File: Python LUT generator for 12AX7 triode tube model using Koren equation
````

````plaintext
Bash Tool: Run the LUT generator to verify it works and see output
````

````plaintext
Bash Tool: Copy all outputs
````

````plaintext
Present File: /mnt/user-data/outputs/tube_plot.png, /mnt/user-data/outputs/tube_lut_gen.py, /mnt/user-data/outputs/lut_params.v, /mnt/user-data/outputs/ip_lut.hex, /mnt/user-data/outputs/dip_dvgk_lut.hex
````

That's the real thing running. Here's what just got generated:

**The plot** shows the actual 12AX7 behavior — left is plate curves (Ip vs voltage for different grid voltages), right is the transfer curve which is literally what happens to your guitar signal. Notice how it curves and flattens — that nonlinearity is the tube sound.

**The numbers:**
- Ip maxes out at 4.4mA which is correct for a 12AX7
- 65,536 entries covering the full operating range
- Both the main LUT and the derivative LUT for the Newton solver

**What you have now:**
- `ip_lut.hex` and `dip_dvgk_lut.hex` — load directly into FPGA BRAM with `$readmemh`
- `lut_params.v` — drop into your Verilog project, has all the constants
- `tube_lut_gen.py` — change `TUBE = "12AU7"` or `"6SL7"` to generate different tubes

Next step is the Verilog — the WDF tree that actually uses these LUTs to process audio. Want to do that now?

> File: /mnt/user-data/outputs/tube_plot.png

> File: /mnt/user-data/outputs/tube_lut_gen.py

> File: /mnt/user-data/outputs/lut_params.v

> File: /mnt/user-data/outputs/ip_lut.hex

> File: /mnt/user-data/outputs/dip_dvgk_lut.hex

## Response:
3/27/2026, 6:08:37 AM

Let me write the core WDF triode stage in Verilog.

````plaintext
View: Check lut_params.v for exact parameter values
````

````plaintext
Create File: WDF triode stage Verilog module - core of the amp modelling
````

````plaintext
Create File: Testbench for wdf_triode - feeds a sine wave and checks output
````

````plaintext
Create File: Python script to analyze testbench output - plots input vs output waveforms and harmonic content
````

````plaintext
Bash Tool: Check if iverilog is available
````

````plaintext
Bash Tool: Compile the Verilog to check for syntax errors
````

````plaintext
Bash Tool: Compile with SystemVerilog enabled
````

````plaintext
Bash Tool: Run simulation
````

````plaintext
Bash Tool: Analyze simulation output
````

Good — it compiled and ran 4933 samples. The gain numbers are way off (83dB, output in thousands of volts) which means the fixed point scaling in the junction math needs calibrating — expected at this stage, the pipeline architecture is right but the resistance ratios in the junctions need proper computation. Let me copy what we have so you can see it.

````plaintext
Bash Tool: Copy Verilog files to outputs
````

````plaintext
Present File: /mnt/user-data/outputs/wdf_triode.v, /mnt/user-data/outputs/wdf_triode_tb.v, /mnt/user-data/outputs/analyze_tb.py
````

Here's where things stand:

**What's working**
- Compiles clean with iverilog
- Simulation runs — 4933 samples of audio processed
- Full pipeline state machine executing: cap → junctions → LUT → Newton → output
- LUT loading from the hex files we generated earlier

**What needs fixing**
The output voltage is wildly wrong — 8000V instead of ~100V. This is a known issue: the junction scattering coefficients (the resistance ratio math) are currently rough approximations. The real WDF junction equations need the exact port resistance ratios computed from the actual component values.

This is actually the next concrete step — fixing the scattering matrix math in `ST_SER_S2`, `ST_SER_S1`, and `ST_PAR_P1`. It's just arithmetic but needs to be precise.

**The full picture of what you have**
- `tube_lut_gen.py` — generates the tube data ✅
- `wdf_triode.v` — the hardware module, architecture correct, scaling needs work
- `wdf_triode_tb.v` — testbench that feeds it 440Hz and captures output
- `analyze_tb.py` — plots waveforms and harmonic content

Want to fix the junction math next, or is this enough to get started with in Claude Code on your own machine?

> File: /mnt/user-data/outputs/wdf_triode.v

> File: /mnt/user-data/outputs/wdf_triode_tb.v

> File: /mnt/user-data/outputs/analyze_tb.py



## Prompt:
3/27/2026, 6:16:29 AM

I went on GitHub and found a lot of stuff for the teensy and other things for wdf. Why is ours better



## Response:
3/27/2026, 6:16:50 AM

Honest answer — right now it isn't, we're behind those projects. Let me look at what actually exists.

````plaintext
Web Search: WDF wave digital filter teensy guitar amp library GitHub 2024 2025
````

> **GitHub - Chowdhury-DSP/chowdsp_wdf: Chowdhury DSP Wave Digital Filters Library** [github.com](https://github.com/Chowdhury-DSP/chowdsp_wdf)
>
> **GitHub - jatinchowdhury18/KlonCentaur: Digital emulation of the Klon Centaur guitar pedal using RNNs, Wave Digital Filters, and more** [github.com](https://github.com/jatinchowdhury18/KlonCentaur)
>
> **GitHub - jatinchowdhury18/WaveDigitalFilters: Circuit Modelling with Wave Digital Filters · GitHub** [github.com](https://github.com/jatinchowdhury18/WaveDigitalFilters)
>
> **GitHub - RT-WDF/rt-wdf_lib: RT-WDF - Real-Time Wave Digital Filter Library** [github.com](https://github.com/RT-WDF/rt-wdf_lib)
>
> **Real-time Wave Digital Filter Library · GitHub** [github.com](https://github.com/RT-WDF)
>
> **WaveDigitalFilters/README.md at master · jatinchowdhury18/WaveDigitalFilters** [github.com](https://github.com/jatinchowdhury18/WaveDigitalFilters/blob/master/README.md)
>
> **GitHub - SMCFY/SMC-WDF: A Real-Time Wave Digital Filter Library for Matlab** [github.com](https://github.com/SMCFY/SMC-WDF)
>
> **GitHub - droosenb/faust-wdf-library** [github.com](https://github.com/droosenb/faust-wdf-library)
>
> **Real-time Wave Digital Filter Library** [github.com](https://github.com/orgs/RT-WDF/repositories)
>
> **GitHub - gusanthon/pywdf: Wave digital filter elements and circuits in python** [github.com](https://github.com/gusanthon/pywdf)
>
Okay, genuinely honest answer:

**The WDF software ecosystem is actually really mature.** chowdsp_wdf is a well maintained header-only C++ library with SIMD acceleration, proper R-type adaptors, and full multiport nonlinearity support. Jatin Chowdhury already built a full Klon Centaur pedal emulation running on a Teensy using WDF. These are serious, polished projects by people with Stanford DSP PhDs.

So on the software side — Teensy/CPU implementations — you'd be competing with experts who have years head start. That's not the right fight.

**But here's the thing that's actually still true:**

Every single one of those projects runs on a CPU — Teensy, desktop plugin, Raspberry Pi. They all have the same fundamental constraint: a processor executes instructions sequentially, one at a time. That limits how many simultaneous voices, how complex the circuit, how low the latency.

FPGA is fundamentally different. The computation runs in parallel hardware, not sequential software. A WDF tree that takes a Teensy 50 microseconds to evaluate could run in 2-3 clock cycles on FPGA — not because it's faster clock-for-clock, but because every node of the tree computes simultaneously.

**So the honest repositioning of what we're building:**

Not "better WDF than Teensy" — it's "WDF in hardware for the first time." The value isn't accuracy, it's what parallel hardware unlocks:

- Sub-millisecond latency — genuinely plug-in-and-play feel, no buffering
- Multiple simultaneous circuit models — 4 triode stages running in parallel, zero extra cost
- Oversampling for free — run at 192kHz or higher without CPU penalty
- Eventually ASIC — if the design works, it could become a dedicated chip

The Teensy projects are the proof that WDF sounds right. Your project is about making it run in hardware. Those are complementary, not competing.

Want to keep going with that framing in mind?



## Prompt:
3/27/2026, 6:18:01 AM

Yes, but can you do more research on why this is or isn't a good idea



## Response:
3/27/2026, 6:18:35 AM

Yeah let me actually dig into this properly rather than just cheerleading.

````plaintext
Web Search: FPGA audio latency vs DSP microcontroller guitar pedal comparison
````

> **Using FPGA for Audio DSP. When we started working on the concept… | by Korora Audio | Medium** [medium.com](https://medium.com/@korora_audio/a-case-study-using-fpga-for-audio-dsp-eab4859bdde2)
>
> **"Design of DSP Guitar Effects with FPGA Implementation" by Connor William Blasie** [rit.edu](https://repository.rit.edu/theses/10361/)
>
> **FPGA Design of Delay-Based Digital Effects for Electric Guitar | SpringerLink** [springer.com](https://link.springer.com/chapter/10.1007/978-3-319-05960-0_20)
>
> **(PDF) FPGA Design of Delay-Based Digital Effects for Electric Guitar** [researchgate.net](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/261775948_FPGA_Design_of_Delay-Based_Digital_Effects_for_Electric_Guitar)
>
> **FPGA Design and Implementation of Electric Guitar Audio Effects - Project Report | PDF | Field Programmable Gate Array | System On A Chip** [scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/384359334/FPGA-Design-and-Implementation-of-Electric-Guitar-Audio-Effects-Project-Report)
>
> **DSP Guitar FX Pedal by Stephen Rock Senior Project** [calpoly.edu](https://digitalcommons.calpoly.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi?article=1614&context=eesp)
>
> **GitHub - jsamarth/GuitarPedalBoard: This is a FPGA Project on Guitar Effects. Usually, guitarists use one guitar effect Stompbox per effect. This project aimed at using the FPGA hardware, which is significantly faster than Microcontrollers, to modulate Guitar signals being fed into the board using Digital Signal Processing.** [github.com](https://github.com/jsamarth/GuitarPedalBoard)
>
> **Can an FPGA beat a DSP on a performance per cost basis for audio processing? - Quora** [quora.com](https://www.quora.com/Can-an-FPGA-beat-a-DSP-on-a-performance-per-cost-basis-for-audio-processing)
>
> **What is best for audio processing DSP processor or FPGA? - Quora** [quora.com](https://www.quora.com/What-is-best-for-audio-processing-DSP-processor-or-FPGA)
>
> **Neural Networks Emulate Any Guitar Pedal For $120 | Hackaday** [hackaday.com](https://hackaday.com/2021/05/30/neural-networks-emulate-any-guitar-pedal-for-120/)
>
````plaintext
Web Search: WDF wave digital filter FPGA disadvantages fixed point precision problems
````

> **(PDF) FIXED-POINT IMPLEMENTATION OF LATTICE WAVE DIGITAL FILTER: COMPARISON AND ERROR ANALYSIS** [researchgate.net](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/280648071_FIXED-POINT_IMPLEMENTATION_OF_LATTICE_WAVE_DIGITAL_FILTER_COMPARISON_AND_ERROR_ANALYSIS)
>
> **FIXED-POINT IMPLEMENTATION OF LATTICE WAVE DIGITAL FILTER: COMPARISON AND ERROR ANALYSIS - Archive ouverte HAL** [hal.science](https://hal.science/hal-01175663/)
>
> **(PDF) Wave Digital Extended Fixed-Point Solvers for Circuits With Multiple One-Port Nonlinearities** [researchgate.net](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/385614385_Wave_Digital_Extended_Fixed-Point_Solvers_for_Circuits_With_Multiple_One-Port_Nonlinearities)
>
> **Wave Digital Filters: Theory and Practice ALFRED FETTWEIS, FELLOW, IEEE** [stanford.edu](https://ccrma.stanford.edu/~jingjiez/portfolio/gtr-amp-sim/pdfs/Wave%20Digital%20Filters%20Theory%20and%20Practice.pdf)
>
> **Design and FPGA implementation of lattice wave digital notch filter with minimal transient duration - Sharma - 2020 - IET Signal Processing - Wiley Online Library** [wiley.com](https://ietresearch.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1049/iet-spr.2020.0074)
>
> **Wave Digital Extended Fixed-Point Solvers for Circuits With Multiple One-Port Nonlinearities | IEEE Journals & Magazine | IEEE Xplore** [ieee.org](https://ieeexplore.ieee.org/document/10745881/)
>
> **comp.arch.fpga | Fixed vs Float ?** [fpgarelated.com](https://www.fpgarelated.com/showthread/comp.arch.fpga/39736-1.php)
>
> **Emulation of analog audio circuits on FPGA using wave digital filters | Request PDF** [researchgate.net](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/313013448_Emulation_of_analog_audio_circuits_on_FPGA_using_wave_digital_filters)
>
> **Radioengineering Design and Implementation of Wave Digital Filters 11** [radioeng.cz](https://www.radioeng.cz/fulltexts/2001/01_03_11_14.pdf)
>
> **Emulation of analog audio circuits on FPGA using wave digital filters | Proceedings of the 2nd International Conference on Communication and Information Processing** [acm.org](https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/3018009.3018028)
>
Okay here's the genuinely balanced picture now that I've actually dug in properly.

**The real case FOR doing this on FPGA**

A commercial pedal company (Korora Audio) actually built a real product on FPGA and measured 250 microseconds total latency analog-in to analog-out, better than any DSP product they evaluated. Their audio pipeline was just five audio clock cycles at 96kHz. That's not theoretical — that's a shipped product.

WDF specifically has a property that helps here — it inherently requires lower precision for multiplier coefficients than other filter structures, which gives extra flexibility in hardware implementation. That's a genuine advantage for fixed-point FPGA math.

And one paper showed a WDF tree structure on FPGA only needs 32 clock cycles to compute a complete sample, running at 18.75 MHz sample rate on a 600MHz clock. On our 27MHz Tang Nano that scales differently but the principle holds — the tree structure maps beautifully to pipelined hardware.

**The real case AGAINST**

FPGA implementation has significantly longer development time compared to microprocessors or DSP chips. Microprocessor/DSP has the quickest development time, low costs, and easier post-development support. This is genuinely true and worth sitting with.

The fixed-point precision problem is real too. Our simulation already showed it — the output was 8000V instead of 100V. Fixed-point WDF implementation requires careful error analysis, and the hardware doesn't always behave as simulated due to quantization and rounding issues. Getting this right is non-trivial work.

**The honest summary**

| | FPGA (us) | Teensy/chowdsp |
|---|---|---|
| Latency | ~250μs possible | ~1-5ms typical |
| Dev speed | Slow, hard | Fast, mature library |
| Circuit accuracy | Same math | Same math |
| Polyphony/parallel | Free | Costs CPU |
| Fixed point pain | Real problem | Not a problem |
| Community/docs | Basically none | Excellent |
| Novelty | Genuine | Already done |

The honest conclusion is this is a hard project that will take real time to get right. The fixed-point scaling alone could take weeks to nail. The latency advantage is real but most guitarists honestly can't tell the difference between 250μs and 1ms.

The reason to still do it isn't "it'll sound better" — it's that nobody has done clean open hardware WDF, and the parallel architecture genuinely unlocks things software can't do. But you should go in knowing it's harder than it looks and the Teensy crowd has a big head start on correctness.

Still want to push forward?
